other_observers: Andrew Spencer, Walter Szeliga, Eric Zorawowicz, Bill & Inez Prather, Nick Komar, many others later in the morning.; Mark Miller found the bird.
specific_location: Underneath and with 100 yards up- and downstream from the Pike Road bridge over Left Hand Creek, about 1 mile east of Hover Road.
description: The bird was clearly a waterthrush, all dull greenish-brown above, streaked with brown below, with a white supercilium; it was about the size of a Hermit Thrush (direct comparison) but chunkier and shorter-tailed, with a long straight bill. The bird's supercilium was completely white and got wider behind the eye, continuing widely all the way to the back of the head, ending where the back begins. The throat was almost completely white, except for a couple of very tiny dark specks that were only really visible through close scope views. The underparts streaking was about the same color as the upperparts coloration, not blackish like on a Northern Waterthrush. The underparts background color was mostly clean white, with a slight tinge of color on the flanks and around the vent. This is the typical Louisiana color: people called it "salmon" or "rusty" but it's actually more like a tan version of yellow; "the color of bathtub scum" was my colorful metaphor. The legs were bright pink.Behaviors: The bird foraged actively on exposed rocks in the middle of a small stream, and also along the banks, frequently underneath overhanging vegetation. It was mostly silent. When pressed, a couple of times it flushed away from the creek and up into dense shrubs in people's yards. The bird was seen to eat a number of good-sized worms. It bobbed its tail frequently. Many people commented on the "u"-shaped bobbing motion--as Andrew Spencer put it, "with more horizontal than vertical motion." I wouldn't have described it this way; the tail never moved separately from the body; whenever the bird tipped its back end down, it would frequently turn its whole body at the same time, and I think this is what people were talking about when they were saying that its tail traced a "U" in the air.Call: The bird gave two chip notes that I heard and recorded. Sound file and sonograms attached.Plumage: adult
